title: Count connected components in an undirected graph.
description: Count connected components in an undirected graph.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Initialize visited=[F,F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Vertex 0 unvisited → count=1, DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 1 visited. Step 4: Vertex 1 visited, skip. Step 5: Vertex 2 visited, skip. Step 6: Vertex 3 unvisited → count=2, DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 4 unvisited → DFS(4): mark visited[4]=T, neighbors: 3 visited. Step 7: Vertex 4 visited, skip. Step 8: Return count=2.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[3,1],3:[2]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Vertex 0 unvisited → count=1, DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 3 unvisited, 1 visited → DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 visited. Step 4: All vertices visited, return count=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform Depth-First Search from each unvisited vertex to explore its entire connected component, marking visited nodes to avoid recounting. This systematically discovers all components by exhaustively traversing the graph.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components in Undirected Graph using Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting connected components via DFS from each unvisited vertex.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires counting all connected components in an undirected graph. A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where there's a path between any two vertices. The brute force DFS approach works by systematically exploring the graph: we maintain a visited set to track explored vertices. For each vertex, if it hasn't been visited, we start a DFS from that vertex to explore its entire connected component, marking all reachable vertices as visited. Each time we initiate a new DFS from an unvisited vertex, we've discovered a new connected component, so we increment our count. The algorithm terminates after processing all vertices. Intuition: Since DFS explores all vertices reachable from a starting point, starting DFS from each unvisited vertex guarantees we discover all components exactly once. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize visited set and component count. 2. For each vertex v: if v not visited, increment count, call DFS(v). 3. In DFS: mark current vertex visited, recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. Time: O(V+E) - each vertex and edge processed once. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS traversal subroutine from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes: Forgetting graph might be disconnected, not marking visited before recursion (causing cycles), using directed graph logic. Real code pattern: def count_components(n, edges): adj = build_adj_list(n, edges); visited = [False]*n; count = 0; for v in range(n): if not visited[v]: count += 1; dfs(v, adj, visited); return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Initialize visited=[F,F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Vertex 0 unvisited → count=1, DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 1 visited. Step 4: Vertex 1 visited, skip. Step 5: Vertex 2 visited, skip. Step 6: Vertex 3 unvisited → count=2, DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 4 unvisited → DFS(4): mark visited[4]=T, neighbors: 3 visited. Step 7: Vertex 4 visited, skip. Step 8: Return count=2.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[3,1],3:[2]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Vertex 0 unvisited → count=1, DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 3 unvisited, 1 visited → DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 visited. Step 4: All vertices visited, return count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal to Explore Connected Component
ladders.desc: Depth-First Search implementation to visit all vertices reachable from a starting vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs DFS from a given starting vertex to explore its entire connected component. The purpose is to mark all vertices reachable from the start as visited, ensuring we don't revisit them. Intuition: DFS recursively explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, naturally covering all connected vertices in an undirected graph. Step-by-step: 1. Mark current vertex as visited. 2. For each neighbor of current vertex: if neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS on neighbor. This ensures we traverse all edges in the component. Time: O(V_c+E_c) for the component being explored. Space: O(V_c) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This is the engine L0 calls for each new component. Connection to L2: This implements the core DFS logic. Common mistakes: Not marking visited before recursion (infinite loop), forgetting to handle disconnected neighbors. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, adj, visited): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor, adj, visited).

examples.input: Start vertex=0, adjacency list: {0:[1,2],1:[0],2:[0,3],3:[2]}, visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T. Step 2: neighbors of 0: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited. Step 3: neighbors of 0: 2 unvisited → DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 3 unvisited → DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 visited. Step 4: All vertices visited.

examples.input: Start vertex=3, adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0],2:[],3:[4],4:[3]}, visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T. Step 2: neighbors of 3: 4 unvisited → DFS(4): mark visited[4]=T, neighbors: 3 visited. Step 3: Stop, vertices 0,1,2 remain unvisited as they're in different components.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DFS Recursive Exploration Pattern
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS pattern to traverse graph vertices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core DFS recursive logic: how to systematically explore vertices using recursion. Intuition: DFS uses a 'depth-first' strategy—explore one path fully before backtracking, which is natural for graph traversal. The key insight is the recursive call explores neighbors, and marking visited prevents cycles. Step-by-step: 1. Base: If vertex visited, return. 2. Mark vertex visited. 3. For each neighbor: if unvisited, recursively call DFS. This creates a chain of calls that explores the graph. Time: O(V+E) for the traversed portion. Space: O(V) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This is the algorithmic pattern L1 implements. Connection to L3: This builds on graph representation. Common mistakes: Infinite recursion if visited check is after recursive call, stack overflow for large graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for u in adj[v]: if not visited[u]: dfs(u).

examples.input: Start at vertex 0, adjacency: 0->[1,2], 1->[0], 2->[0], visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T. Step 2: neighbors: 1 unvisited → dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited. Step 3: back to dfs(0): next neighbor 2 unvisited → dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 0 visited. Step 4: Done.

examples.input: Start at vertex 0, adjacency: 0->[1], 1->[0,2], 2->[1,3], 3->[2], visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbor 1 unvisited → dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors: 1 visited, 3 unvisited → dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors: 2 visited. Step 2: Backtrack to completion.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Constructing and using adjacency list representation for undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: This level covers representing an undirected graph as an adjacency list, which is essential for efficient DFS. Intuition: An adjacency list stores each vertex's neighbors in a list, providing O(degree) access to neighbors, optimal for traversal. For undirected graphs, each edge (u,v) adds v to u's list and u to v's list. Step-by-step construction: 1. Initialize empty list for each vertex. 2. For each edge [u,v]: append v to adj[u], append u to adj[v]. This creates a symmetric representation. Time to build: O(V+E). Space: O(V+E). Connection to L2: DFS uses this adjacency list to access neighbors. Connection to L4: This uses list appends as basic operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, index errors with 0-based vs 1-based vertices. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0] → [[1],[],[]]; append 0 to adj[1] → [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: append 2 to adj[1] → [[1],[0,2],[]]; append 1 to adj[2] → [[1],[0,2],[1]]. Step 4: Result: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Neighbors in a Graph
ladders.desc: Accessing and processing all neighbors of a vertex from adjacency list.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of iterating through all neighbors of a vertex, which is central to graph traversal. Intuition: In graph algorithms, we often need to examine all vertices adjacent to a current vertex to decide where to go next. Using an adjacency list, this is simply looping through the list associated with that vertex. Step-by-step: Given adjacency list adj and vertex v, for each neighbor in adj[v]: perform some operation (e.g., check if visited, recursive call). This is O(degree(v)) time. Connection to L3: This operation uses the adjacency list built in L3. Connection to L5: This relies on list iteration basics. Common mistakes: Modifying list while iterating, assuming neighbors are sorted. Real code pattern: for neighbor in adj[vertex]: # process neighbor.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access adj[1] which is [0,3]. Step 2: Iterate: first neighbor 0, then neighbor 3.
Step 1: Access adj[0] which is []. Step 2: No neighbors to iterate over.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Vertex and Edge Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding vertices, edges, and undirected graphs at a fundamental level.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces basic graph concepts: vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In an undirected graph, edges have no direction—if there's an edge between vertex A and B, you can travel both ways. A graph is defined by a set of vertices V and edges E. Intuition: Think of vertices as points and edges as lines connecting them, like a social network where people are vertices and friendships are edges. Key properties: Vertices are typically labeled 0 to n-1, edges are pairs (u,v). This is prerequisite for building adjacency lists and traversal. Connection to L4: This provides the raw data (vertices and edges) that L4 operations process. Common mistakes: Confusing directed vs undirected edges, off-by-one in vertex numbering. Real code pattern: Represent vertices as integers, edges as list of pairs.

examples.output: Graph with 3 vertices and 2 edges forming a path
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1) connects vertex 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge (1,2) connects vertex 1 and 2. Step 4: Visual: 0—1—2, undirected so can go 0↔1 and 1↔2.

examples.output: Graph with 4 vertices and 2 edges forming two disconnected components
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge (0,1) connects 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge (2,3) connects 2 and 3. Step 4: No edges between {0,1} and {2,3}, so two separate components.

approaches.name: BFS Traversal O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use Breadth-First Search instead of DFS to explore connected components level by level from each unvisited vertex. This approach processes nodes in layers rather than going deep first.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components Using BFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count connected components in an undirected graph using BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph. A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where there's a path between any two vertices. The BFS approach works by systematically exploring the graph from each unvisited vertex, marking all reachable vertices as visited, and incrementing a counter for each new component discovered. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes layer by layer from a starting point, which naturally identifies all vertices in that component. We iterate through all vertices, and for each unvisited vertex, we perform BFS to visit all vertices in its component. The time complexity is O(V+E) because we visit each vertex and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and BFS queue. This connects to L1 by using the BFS traversal subroutine to explore components. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs properly, forgetting to mark the starting vertex as visited before BFS, or using directed graph logic. Real code pattern: Initialize visited array, component count = 0. For each vertex, if not visited, increment count, perform BFS from that vertex to mark all connected vertices as visited. Return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited = [False, False, False, False, False], count = 0. Step 2: Vertex 0 not visited, count = 1. BFS from 0: queue = [0], visited[0]=True. Process 0: neighbors 1, queue = [1], visited[1]=True. Process 1: neighbors 0,2, queue = [2], visited[2]=True. Process 2: neighbors 1, queue empty. Step 3: Vertex 1 visited, skip. Vertex 2 visited, skip. Vertex 3 not visited, count = 2. BFS from 3: queue = [3], visited[3]=True. Process 3: neighbors 4, queue = [4], visited[4]=True. Process 4: neighbors 3, queue empty. Step 4: Vertex 4 visited, skip. All vertices visited, return count = 2.

Step 1: visited = [False, False, False, False], count = 0. Step 2: Vertex 0 not visited, count = 1. BFS from 0: queue = [0], visited[0]=True. Process 0: neighbors 1, queue = [1], visited[1]=True. Process 1: neighbors 0,2, queue = [2], visited[2]=True. Process 2: neighbors 1,3, queue = [3], visited[3]=True. Process 3: neighbors 2, queue empty. Step 3: All other vertices visited, return count = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal from a Starting Vertex
ladders.desc: Perform BFS to visit all vertices reachable from a given starting vertex in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements BFS traversal from a specific vertex to explore all vertices in its connected component. The intuition is that BFS uses a queue to process vertices in layers, ensuring we visit all vertices at increasing distances from the start. This is efficient for component exploration because it systematically marks all reachable vertices. Step-by-step: Initialize a queue with the starting vertex, mark it as visited. While queue is not empty, dequeue a vertex, process it (e.g., mark visited), and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component, space is O(V) for queue and visited tracking. This feeds into L0 by being called for each unvisited vertex to count components. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (leading to duplicates), or using stack instead of queue. Real code pattern: bfs(start, graph, visited): queue = [start], visited[start]=True. While queue: vertex = queue.pop(0), for neighbor in graph[vertex]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor]=True, queue.append(neighbor).

examples.input: Start vertex: 0, graph: adjacency list {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1]}, visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [0], visited[0]=True. Step 2: queue not empty, dequeue 0, neighbors: 1, visited[1]=False, so queue = [1], visited[1]=True. Step 3: dequeue 1, neighbors: 0 (visited), 2 (not visited), so queue = [2], visited[2]=True. Step 4: dequeue 2, neighbors: 1 (visited), queue empty. Final visited = [True, True, True].

examples.input: Start vertex: 3, graph: {3:[4],4:[3]}, visited initially all False for vertices 3,4
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [3], visited[3]=True. Step 2: dequeue 3, neighbors: 4, visited[4]=False, so queue = [4], visited[4]=True. Step 3: dequeue 4, neighbors: 3 (visited), queue empty. Final visited = [True, True].

testCases.input: Start: 0, graph: {0:[1],1:[0],2:[3],3:[2]}, visited: [False, False, False, False]
Start: 2, graph: {0:[1],1:[0],2:[3],3:[2]}, visited: [False, False, False, False]
Start: 0, graph: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}, visited: [False, False, False, False]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over Vertices and Counting Components
ladders.desc: The central technique of iterating through all vertices and using a visited array to track components.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of counting components by iterating over each vertex and using a visited array to avoid recounting. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, each connected component can be identified by starting from any unvisited vertex within it. By maintaining a visited array, we ensure each vertex is processed exactly once. The algorithm: Initialize a visited array of size V with False, and a count = 0. For each vertex from 0 to V-1, if not visited, increment count (new component found) and mark all vertices in this component as visited (using BFS from L1). This works because visiting all reachable vertices from a start covers the entire component. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once; space is O(V) for visited array. This connects to L1 by calling BFS for each new component. Common mistakes include not resetting visited array, or using incorrect iteration bounds. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V, count = 0. For v in range(V): if not visited[v]: count += 1, bfs(v, graph, visited). Return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited = [False, False, False], count = 0. Step 2: v=0, not visited, count=1, call BFS from 0 marks visited[0,1,2]=True. Step 3: v=1 visited, skip. v=2 visited, skip. Return count=1.

Step 1: visited = [False, False, False, False], count = 0. Step 2: v=0, not visited, count=1, BFS from 0 marks visited[0,1]=True. Step 3: v=1 visited, skip. v=2 not visited, count=2, BFS from 2 marks visited[2,3]=True. Step 4: v=3 visited, skip. Return count=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent an undirected graph using an adjacency list for efficient neighbor access.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing the graph in a way that supports BFS traversal. An adjacency list is a dictionary or list of lists where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices. For an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) is added twice: once in u's list and once in v's list. The intuition is that this representation allows O(1) access to neighbors, which is efficient for BFS. Step-by-step: Given number of vertices V and list of edges, initialize an empty adjacency list of size V. For each edge [u,v], append v to list[u] and u to list[v]. Time to build is O(E), space is O(V+E). This connects to L2 by providing the graph input for iteration. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate edges, or using directed graph representation. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]. For u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v), graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: graph[0].append(1) -> [1], graph[1].append(0) -> [0]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: graph[1].append(2) -> [0,2], graph[2].append(1) -> [1]. Final graph: [[1],[0,2],[1]].

Step 1: graph = [[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: graph[0].append(1) -> [1], graph[1].append(0) -> [0]. Step 3: Edge [2,3]: graph[2].append(3) -> [3], graph[3].append(2) -> [2]. Final graph: [[1],[0],[3],[2]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement basic queue operations (enqueue and dequeue) used in BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a queue to manage vertices in BFS. A queue is a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure. In BFS, we enqueue vertices when discovered and dequeue to process them in order. The intuition is that this ensures we explore vertices level by level from the start. Step-by-step: Initialize an empty list as queue. Enqueue: append item to end of list. Dequeue: remove and return first item from list (index 0). Time complexity: O(1) for enqueue, O(n) for dequeue if using list (but can be O(1) with collections.deque). Space is O(n) for queue storage. This connects to L3 by being used in BFS to traverse the adjacency list. Common mistakes include using stack operations (LIFO) or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: queue = [], enqueue: queue.append(item), dequeue: item = queue.pop(0).

examples.input: Enqueue 0, enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [], enqueue 0 -> queue = [0]. Step 2: enqueue 1 -> queue = [0,1]. Step 3: dequeue -> return 0, queue = [1]. Step 4: enqueue 2 -> queue = [1,2]. Step 5: dequeue -> return 1, queue = [2]. Step 6: dequeue -> return 2, queue = [].

examples.input: Enqueue A, enqueue B, dequeue, enqueue C, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [], enqueue A -> [A]. Step 2: enqueue B -> [A,B]. Step 3: dequeue -> return A, queue = [B]. Step 4: enqueue C -> [B,C]. Step 5: dequeue -> return B, queue = [C].

testCases.input: Operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, enqueue 2, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Operations: enqueue A, dequeue, enqueue B, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 10, enqueue 20, dequeue, enqueue 30, dequeue, dequeue

testCases.expected: Dequeued: X, error on second dequeue

testCases.input: Operations: enqueue 7, enqueue 8, dequeue, enqueue 9, dequeue, enqueue 10, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue
Operations: enqueue 100, enqueue 200, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Operations: enqueue A, enqueue B, enqueue C, dequeue, dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Graphs and Vertices
ladders.desc: Basic concept of a graph as a collection of vertices and edges.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a graph is a mathematical structure consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In an undirected graph, edges have no direction, meaning if vertex A is connected to vertex B, then B is also connected to A. Vertices are typically represented as integers starting from 0. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, such as social networks or road maps. For example, vertices can represent people, and edges represent friendships. This foundational idea is essential for later levels because BFS operates on this structure. There are no complex algorithms here; just understanding that a graph with V vertices can have edges listed as pairs. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected graphs. Real code pattern: Think of vertices as numbers 0 to V-1, edges as list of pairs like [[0,1], [1,2]].

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects vertex 0 and vertex 1. Step 3: Edge [1,2] connects vertex 1 and vertex 2. Step 4: Since undirected, 0 is connected to 1, 1 is connected to 0 and 2, 2 is connected to 1. Visual representation: a line from 0 to 1 to 2.

Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge [2,3] connects 2 and 3. Step 4: No edges between {0,1} and {2,3}, so two separate groups. Visual: one line between 0 and 1, another between 2 and 3.

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) O(V+E·α(V))
approaches.summary: Initialize each vertex as its own set, then union connected vertices using the edges. The number of distinct roots after processing all edges equals the number of connected components.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Connected Components Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting connected components in an undirected graph using Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to count connected components in an undirected graph using Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union). The intuition is that initially each vertex is its own component. As we process each edge connecting two vertices, we union their sets. After processing all edges, the number of distinct roots (representatives) equals the number of connected components. This works because union operations merge vertices that are connected, and find operations identify which component each vertex belongs to. The algorithm: 1. Initialize parent array where each vertex is its own parent. 2. Initialize rank array for union by rank optimization. 3. For each edge (u, v), call union(u, v). 4. Count distinct roots by calling find on each vertex. Time complexity is O(V + E·α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space is O(V). This connects to L1 by using the union and find operations as building blocks. Common mistakes include not using path compression or union by rank, which leads to worse time complexity, or incorrectly counting components by checking parent array directly instead of using find (which may not have full path compression).

examples.trace: Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1) with rank 0=0, set parent[0]=1, rank[1]=1. Now parent=[1,1,2,3,4]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1, find(2)=2, union(1,2) with rank 1>0, set parent[2]=1. Now parent=[1,1,1,3,4]. Edge [3,4]: find(3)=3, find(4)=4, union(3,4) with rank 0=0, set parent[3]=4, rank[4]=1. Now parent=[1,1,1,4,4]. Count distinct roots: find(0)=1, find(1)=1, find(2)=1, find(3)=4, find(4)=4. Distinct roots: {1,4} → count=2.

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1) with rank 0=0, set parent[0]=1, rank[1]=1. Now parent=[1,1,2,3]. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union(2,3) with rank 0=0, set parent[2]=3, rank[3]=1. Now parent=[1,1,3,3]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1, find(2)=3, union(1,3) with rank 1=1, set parent[3]=1, rank[1]=2. Now parent=[1,1,3,1]. Count distinct roots: find(0)=1, find(1)=1, find(2)=1, find(3)=1. Distinct roots: {1} → count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union and Find Operations
ladders.desc: Implement the union and find functions with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the two core operations of Union-Find: find (to determine the root/representative of a set) and union (to merge two sets). The intuition is that we maintain a parent array where each element points to its parent, and the root points to itself. Find uses path compression: while finding the root, it flattens the tree by pointing nodes directly to the root, making future finds faster. Union uses union by rank: it attaches the shorter tree under the taller one to keep the tree balanced, which also optimizes time. Pseudocode: find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. union(x, y): rootX = find(x); rootY = find(y); if rootX != rootY: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY; else if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]: parent[rootY] = rootX; else: parent[rootY] = rootX; rank[rootX] += 1. Time per operation is O(α(n)) amortized. Space is O(n). This connects to L0 as these operations are used to process edges and count components. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly (e.g., using iterative find without compression) or forgetting to update rank during union.

examples.input: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2), union(1,3)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank 0=0, set parent[0]=1, rank[1]=1 → parent=[1,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,1,0,0,0]. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank 0=0, set parent[2]=3, rank[3]=1 → parent=[1,1,3,3,4], rank=[0,1,0,1,0]. find(0): parent[0]=1, parent[1]=1 → return 1. find(2): parent[2]=3, parent[3]=3 → return 3. union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, rank 1=1, set parent[3]=1, rank[1]=2 → parent=[1,1,3,1,4], rank=[0,2,0,1,0].

examples.input: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Operations: union(0,2), union(1,3), find(0), find(1), union(2,3)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank 0=0, set parent[0]=2, rank[2]=1 → parent=[2,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,1,0]. union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, rank 0=0, set parent[1]=3, rank[3]=1 → parent=[2,3,2,3], rank=[0,0,1,1]. find(0): parent[0]=2, parent[2]=2 → return 2. find(1): parent[1]=3, parent[3]=3 → return 3. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank 1=1, set parent[3]=2, rank[2]=2 → parent=[2,3,2,2], rank=[0,0,2,1].

testCases.input: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]; union(0,1); union(0,2); union(0,3); find(3)
parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]; union(0,2); union(1,3); union(2,3); find(1)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sets via Edges
ladders.desc: Process edges to union vertices and track distinct sets.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central technique of using edges to merge sets in Union-Find. The intuition is that each edge represents a connection between two vertices, so by unioning their sets, we group all vertices that are connected into the same set. Initially, each vertex is in its own set. As we iterate through edges, for each edge (u, v), we call union(u, v). After processing all edges, vertices in the same connected component will have the same root. This works because union operations are transitive: if u is connected to v and v to w, then u, v, w end up in the same set. The algorithm: initialize Union-Find for V vertices; for each edge in edges: union(edge[0], edge[1]); count distinct roots by applying find to each vertex. Time is O(E·α(V)) for union operations plus O(V·α(V)) for counting. Space is O(V). This connects to L1 by using union and find functions. Common mistakes include not iterating over all edges or incorrectly assuming the graph is connected.

examples.trace: Initialize Union-Find: parent=[0,1,2,3]. Edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → parent=[1,1,2,3]. Edge [2,3]: union(2,3) → parent=[1,1,3,3]. Distinct roots: find(0)=1, find(1)=1, find(2)=3, find(3)=3 → sets: {0,1} and {2,3}.

Initialize: parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → parent=[1,1,2,3,4]. Edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → parent=[1,1,1,3,4]. Edge [3,4]: union(3,4) → parent=[1,1,1,4,4]. Distinct roots: find(0)=1, find(1)=1, find(2)=1, find(3)=4, find(4)=4 → sets: {0,1,2} and {3,4}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint Set Representation
ladders.desc: Represent sets using parent pointers and rank for optimization.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the data structure used in Union-Find: representing disjoint sets as trees where each node has a parent pointer, and the root represents the set. The intuition is that we can group elements into sets by having them point to a common root. We use two arrays: parent (where parent[i] is the parent of i, with root having parent[i]=i) and rank (height of tree rooted at i, used for union by rank). Initially, each element is its own root with rank 0. This representation allows efficient find and union operations. Pseudocode for initialization: parent = [i for i in range(n)]; rank = [0] * n. Time to initialize is O(n), space is O(n). This connects to L2 as it provides the structure to merge sets via edges. Common mistakes include not initializing parent correctly or confusing rank with size.

examples.trace: For n=5, initialize parent array: index 0 to 4, each points to itself → parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Initialize rank array with zeros → rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. This represents 5 disjoint sets: {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}, {4}.
For n=3, parent array: indices 0,1,2 each point to themselves → parent=[0,1,2]. rank array: all zeros → rank=[0,0,0]. Sets: {0}, {1}, {2}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Root with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement find operation to determine set representative with optimization.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the find operation, which determines the root (representative) of a set for a given element. The intuition is that by recursively following parent pointers until reaching a node that points to itself (the root), we can identify which set an element belongs to. Path compression optimizes this by making nodes point directly to the root during the find, flattening the tree for future operations. Pseudocode: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. This is recursive; an iterative version is also possible. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression. Space is O(1) per call, but recursion uses stack space. This connects to L3 as it operates on the parent array. Common mistakes include not using path compression (e.g., only returning parent[x] without recursion) or infinite recursion if parent is not initialized correctly.

examples.trace: Start: parent=[1,1,2,3,4]. find(0): parent[0]=1 ≠ 0, so call find(1). find(1): parent[1]=1, return 1. Back to find(0): set parent[0]=1, return 1. After: parent=[1,1,2,3,4] (no change as 0 already pointed to root).
Start: parent=[2,2,2,3,4]. find(1): parent[1]=2 ≠ 1, call find(2). find(2): parent[2]=2, return 2. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=2, return 2. After: parent=[2,2,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets and Connectivity
ladders.desc: Understand sets and how elements can be grouped as connected.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the concept of sets and connectivity. A set is a collection of distinct elements. In graph terms, vertices can be grouped into sets based on connectivity: two vertices are in the same set if there is a path between them (i.e., they are connected). Initially, with no edges, each vertex is in its own set. When an edge connects two vertices, their sets merge. This is intuitive: if you know person A is friends with B, and B with C, then A, B, C are all in the same friend group (set). The goal is to count how many such groups exist after all connections are considered. This concept uses basic ideas of grouping and relationships. No pseudocode here; it's conceptual. Time and space are not applicable. This connects to L4 as it motivates why we need operations like find to determine set membership. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists or not understanding transitive connectivity.

examples.trace: Start: each vertex in its own set: {A}, {B}, {C}, {D}. Edge A-B: merge sets of A and B → {A,B}, {C}, {D}. Edge C-D: merge sets of C and D → {A,B}, {C,D}. Final sets: {A,B} and {C,D}.
Start: sets: {X}, {Y}, {Z}. Edge X-Y: merge → {X,Y}, {Z}. Edge Y-Z: merge sets of Y and Z → {X,Y,Z}. Final set: {X,Y,Z}.
